data.title: The Dignity Act is a realistic step toward immigration reform and justice
data.description:
The Dignity Act — a bipartisan effort aimed at strengthening immigration control and border security while simultaneously protecting those affected by recent immigration actions against deportations — has come under fire from Republicans. 

Rep. Brandon Gill (R-Texas) called it, “a mass amnesty” that “would constitute a terrible betrayal of our voters.” Rep. Andy Ogles (R-Tenn.) equates it to “Treason.” Rep. Randy Fine (R-Fla) wrote that “Americans voted for mass deportations, NOT mass amnesty,” and that the act “should never see the light of day on the House floor.”

Neither liberals nor conservatives will completely agree with everything in this bill — I certainly don’t. Yet the Dignity Act offers the most plausible path toward meaningful immigration reform and justice.

To those on the political right: Yes, Trump ran on “mass deportations.” It was a core election promise. However, it is time to accept reality: Like his “no wars” pledge, it was a lie. There will be no widespread campaign that succeeds in detaining and deporting everyone living in the U.S. without legal permission for at least three reasons.

First, it’s too costly. The Department of Homeland Security estimates that it takes an average of $17,121 to “arrest, detain, and remove” each immigrant living in the U.S. illegally. The Immigration Research Initiative reports that there are as many as 14 million undocumented immigrants in the U.S., although Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.), a Dignity Act cosponsor, puts the figure at over 25 million.

Using these figures, mass deportation would cost anywhere between $208 billion and $428 billion — between one-fourth and one-half of this year’s Pentagon budget. But in fact, the real cost may be higher than that.

Second, mass deportations would harm the U.S. economy. The American Immigration Council estimates that it would lower GDP by $1.1 trillion to $1.7 trillion. The Joint Economic Committee predicts that it would cause major labor shortages in construction, agriculture, healthcare and hospitality, while also contributing to higher prices.

Third, the sort of aggressive enforcement that would be required to deport such large numbers is unsustainably violent. In just a year, immigration enforcement has assaulted, tased, tear-gassed, injured, shot and killed both citizens and noncitizens. Even if some of the incidents could be justified, these actions have collectively built up the opposition to ICE’s very existence into something half of Americans support. Mass deportation will only make Homeland Security and ICE more unpopular, which isn’t what conservatives want.

Part of the problem is that, despite Republicans fearmongering about “violent criminal illegal aliens,” people without permanent legal status commit fewer crimes than U.S. citizens, according to the CATO Institute. Any mass deportation campaign will disproportionately target non-criminals, undermining public support. Moreover, as we saw in Minneapolis, it will inevitably harm U.S. citizens and spark protests.

The Dignity Act does not offer mass deportation, but it does achieve several attainable conservative goals. These include bolstered border and port of entry security, increased criminal penalties for non-citizens found guilty of illegally voting and civil penalties for employers who hire unauthorized workers. The Dignity program imposes a 1 percent fee on the income earned from participants to help pay for these costs, sparing the deficit. Despite conservative criticisms, the Dignity Act prioritizes many of their goals.

To those on the political left, the Dignity Act is not amnesty. It does not provide a pathway to citizenship, and it bans those without permanent legal status from access to benefits and entitlement programs. There are also worrying provisions, such as unnecessarily invasive biometric screening mandates and overly punitive fines on immigrants as restitution. Also, participation in the Dignity Program must be renewed every seven years, creating a recurring cycle of vulnerability for enrollees at the end of each period.

Despite this, the act achieves a number of important goals. It codifies a version of the Dream Act, providing permanent legal status to those who were brought to the U.S. illegally as children, and a pathway toward lawful permanent residency. It also offers millions of hardworking, law-abiding immigrants with protections against being forcibly removed from their homes and separated from their loved ones.

This is a major win that stops the cruelest aspects of Trump’s immigration policies and affords those on the left the opportunity to build upon these protections going forward. More importantly, it will provide many people with the legal protections they need to live without fear, empowering them to advocate on their own behalf. 

A major component missing from the public immigration debate is the voice of those without permanent legal status. This is unsurprising, given the risks involved if they share their stories. This absence, however, allows politicians and the media to shape public perception about immigrants in ways that don’t reflect reality. Legal protections give them the opportunity to shape their own narrative without fear of punishment.

The Dignity Act is not a perfect solution. However, it offers meaningful gains and protections without jeopardizing its political viability. It offers hope while being realistic. In our highly polarized political landscape, we cannot allow the good to be the enemy of the great, especially when so many livelihoods are at stake.

Jordan Liz is an associate professor of philosophy at San Jose State University.

data.title: READ: Supreme Court ruling on Voting Rights Act in Louisiana v. Callais
data.description:
The Supreme Court, in a 6-3 decision along ideological lines, struck down Louisiana’s congressional map on Wednesday that added a second majority-Black district, a decision that carries seismic implications for the future of the Voting Rights Act. 

Louisiana’s legal saga thrust the state into the center of conservatives’ push to weaken a central provision of the 1965 law that has long enabled advocacy groups to force new majority-minority districts. 

The decision does not strike down the provision entirely. Justice Samuel Alito cast it as an “update” to the framework that has governed Voting Rights Act cases for decades, saying lower courts had stretched it too far. Justice Elena Kagan led the dissent, which she read aloud in court.

Read the opinion below.

24-109_21o3Download

data.title: Supreme Court rules on Voting Rights Act, striking down Louisiana map
data.description:
The Supreme Court in a 6-3 decision along ideological lines struck down Louisiana’s congressional map on Wednesday that added a second majority-Black district, a decision that carries seismic implications for the future of the Voting Rights Act. 

Louisiana’s legal saga thrust the state into the center of conservatives’ push to weaken a central provision of the 1965 law that has long enabled advocacy groups to force new majority-minority districts. 

The decision does not strike down the provision entirely. Justice Samuel Alito cast it as an “update” to the framework that has governed Voting Rights Act cases for decades, saying lower courts had stretched it too far.

And under that proper framework, Alito agreed the Constitution does not tolerate Louisiana’s new majority-Black district, which the state intentionally added after a lower court ruled a design with only one violated the landmark law.

“That map is an unconstitutional gerrymander, and its use would violate the plaintiffs’ constitutional rights,” Alito wrote. 

Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act prevents election practices that deny equal access to the political process based on race. 

For decades, groups like the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People and the American Civil Liberties Union have filed Section 2 lawsuits to prompt states to redraw their maps with new boundaries that boost minority voting power. 

The Supreme Court’s decision claws back their ability to do so by limiting court-ordered redistricting that intentionally uses race as a factor. 

It carries major implications for future elections that extend well beyond Louisiana’s map. States across the south and elsewhere have drawn majority-minority districts to comply with the Voting Rights Act that could now be at risk. Left-leaning and civil rights groups have been bracing for a new wave of litigation. 

It adds a new layer to what was already a busy redistricting cycle in advance of the 2026 midterms, when Republicans hope to hold onto their slim House majority. Texas passed a new map with Republican pickup opportunities, sparking a mid-decade arms race that has led states like California and Missouri to follow. 

Louisiana’s redistricting fight dates back to the 2020 census, ping ponging through the courts ever since and leaving the state frustrated with the never-ending litigation. It took on increased national attention as the justices began using the case to settle broader redistricting issues. 

The battle commenced when then-Gov. John Bel Edwards (D) vetoed the Republican-controlled Legislature’s original design, which only included one majority-Black congressional district. The legislature overrode the veto, putting the map into effect. 

Black voters and civil rights groups sued. They convinced a lower court the design likely violated Section 2 by diluting Black voting power, but the Supreme Court temporarily let Louisiana use it for the 2022 midterms as the battle progressed.  

After the election, the justices resolved a similar case in Alabama, keeping Section 2 intact. It meant Louisiana’s map could not survive, effectively forcing the drawing of a second majority-Black district. 

Staring down the prospect that a judge would step in to draw the new boundaries, Louisiana Republicans begrudgingly got to work. They passed a new design that added a second majority-Black district. 

It stretches from the state’s northwestern corner, near Shreveport, and snakes southeastward to Baton Rouge. Louisiana leaders have emphasized they drew it to preserve the state’s powerful Republican incumbents: Speaker Mike Johnson, House Majority Leader Steve Scalise and Appropriations Committee member Julia Letlow.  

The lawsuit now at the Supreme Court was brought by a group of self-described “non-African American” voters.  

In challenging the new design, they argued Louisiana went too far in boosting Black voting representation, alleging the design amounted to an unconstitutional racial gerrymander. 

The Supreme Court took up the case after a lower court ruled in the challengers’ favor. In a rare move, the justices heard re-argument in the case before reaching the decision.

data.title: Trump discusses extended Iran blockade with energy executives at White House
data.description:
President Trump discussed domestic and international energy markets during a meeting with industry executives and other officials at the White House on Tuesday, according to a White House official. 

The official told The Hill that Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent hosted the meeting which was also attended by Vice President Vance and White House chief of staff Susie Wiles.

The group discussed steps Trump has taken to alleviate global oil markets that have been rattled in the wake of the Iran war. Potential steps to continue the U.S. naval blockade on Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz was also discussed, along with ways its impact on U.S. consumers could be minimized. 

The White House official also noted that the oil executives present praised Trump’s actions on U.S. energy dominance, pointing to initiatives like the Jones Act and Defense Production Act. 

The meeting comes as the U.S. and Israel’s war in Iran is set to pass the 60-day mark — as the conflict continues to wreak havoc on domestic and global energy markets. 

The U.S. Navy’s blockade, which began more than two weeks ago, has stopped some ships from entering the key oil-transporting waterway. Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps initially restricted shipping in the strait in response to the initial wave of U.S. and Israeli strikes in late February. 

In the early hours of Wednesday morning, Trump posted an altered image to Truth Social of him holding a gun and bombs going off in a desert setting behind him with a message to Iran. 

“Iran can’t get their act together,” the president wrote. “They don’t know how to sign a nonnuclear deal. They better get smart soon.” 

Gas prices rose more than five cents from Tuesday to Wednesday, to nearly $4.23, according to AAA. That is the highest mark since April 2022, when the national average peaked at $4.11 in the early months of Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

Meanwhile, Brent crude, the international oil benchmark, sat at $116 per barrel on Wednesday.

data.title: Pam Bondi sets new date to testify before Congress on Epstein files
data.description:
Former Attorney General Pam Bondi is set to testify before Congress about the Epstein files on May 29 after she failed to appear for a previously scheduled deposition. 

The House Oversight Committee confirmed the appearance, an arrangement made after discussions with Bondi’s personal attorney. 

Bondi was subpoenaed to testify after a surprise motion in March by Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), who gained the support of four GOP colleagues and all Democrats on the panel. 

Democrats on the committee had been threatening an effort to hold Bondi in contempt for not appearing before the panel earlier this month.  

Developing.

data.title: Supreme Court sides with NJ faith-based pregnancy centers in subpoena fight
data.description:
The Supreme Court unanimously sided with a group of faith-based pregnancy centers on Wednesday in their fight with New Jersey’s Democratic attorney general, who is investigating allegations the centers may have misled donors to believe they support abortions.  

Justice Neil Gorsuch said First Choice Women’s Resource Centers can proceed with its First Amendment challenge to the office’s subpoena in federal court. Lower judges ruled the centers hadn’t shown a chilling effect yet, so they needed to wait until New Jersey took more aggressive enforcement.  

“From its allegations and declarations, and given our many and longstanding precedents in the area and reasonable inferences about third party behavior, First Choice has established that the Attorney General’s demand for private donor information injures the group’s First Amendment associational rights,” Gorsuch wrote. 

First Choice went to court after then-New Jersey Attorney General Matthew Platkin (D) opened an investigation into whether the group violated the state’s consumer fraud laws.  

The group, which describes itself as “a faith-based nonprofit that advocates pro-life views,” does not offer abortions at the five facilities it operates in New Jersey. The subpoena seeks materials First Choice used to solicit donors and information about people who’ve contributed.     

The battle has rippled through various courts ever since. It reached the Supreme Court after an appeals panel ruled that First Choice’s First Amendment defense wasn’t ripe yet and the group must proceed in state court.  

At the center of the case is whether Platkin’s subpoena was “self-executing,” meaning if it is automatically legally enforceable by virtue of sending it.    

New Jersey argued it isn’t, and First Choice can’t sue in federal court until the state takes additional enforcement steps. But the group said it was already causing a chilling effect for its donors and it may be prevented later from entering the federal courthouse doors on procedural grounds.  

First Choice’s appeal was supported by free speech groups, libertarian and conservative think tanks, 19 Republican state attorneys general and religious groups like the U.S. Conference of Catholic Bishops and The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.  

New Jersey’s position garnered support from the National Association of Counties, the Federation of State Medical Boards 20 Democratic state attorneys general. 

Platkin left the role of New Jersey attorney general in January as the state’s new governor took office.

data.title: McCabe on new Comey indictment: DOJ has 'real work to do'
data.description:
Former Deputy FBI Director Andrew McCabe on Tuesday slammed the Justice Department (DOJ) for bringing charges against his former boss, James Comey, for a second time, arguing there are better uses of the department’s time and resources.

“They actually have real work to do,” McCabe told CNN’s Anderson Cooper. “It boggles my mind that they’re still able to find people inside the department and the FBI, for that matter, if we take the acting attorney general at his word, who would actually spend their time working on this, this thing.”

“But do they have a choice?” Cooper asked, to which McCabe responded, “We all have choices at the end of the day.”

A federal grand jury in North Carolina indicted Comey on Tuesday on two charges for allegedly threatening the life of President Trump, stemming from a social media post of seashells arranged to read “86 47.”

“Threatening the life of the president of the United States will never be tolerated by the Department of Justice,” acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said at a Tuesday press conference announcing the indictment. “Over the past year, this department has charged dozens of cases involving threats against all sorts of individuals.”

“We take these seriously,” Blanche added. “Every single one of them.”

Comey posted the photo last May but deleted it after facing backlash from critics, including the president, who interpreted the message as a call for assassination. The former FBI director denied that the post was intended to incite harm, saying he believed it was a “political message” and did not know the numbers could carry a violent connotation.

Comey voluntarily met with Secret Service agents a day after sharing the post.

“If there were any legitimacy, if there was even a legitimate argument that that statement was a threat, do you actually think the Secret Service, after having interviewed him the day after the threat was allegedly made, would have allowed Jim Comey to live his life walking around free, doing nothing for the last year?” McCabe asked rhetorically on Tuesday.

“Do you think this guy is actually a legitimate threat to the President of the United States, and you waited a year to arrest him?” he continued, labeling the DOJ’s latest attempt at prosecution “an absolute fraud.”

The term “86” is commonly used slang, often in restaurants, meaning to get rid of, discard or to refuse service to someone or something — similar to the term “nix.”

The indictment marks the second effort by the Trump administration to prosecute Comey. He was indicted last September for making false statements and obstruction of justice stemming from 2020 congressional testimony about FBI leaks, but the charges were dismissed over the unlawful appointment of the prosecutor who secured them.

The Trump administration has appealed in that case.

McCabe, who Trump has frequently targeted due to his role in the early stages of the 2016 election interference probe, said on Tuesday that he does not expect the president to be deterred from pursuing his “thirst for retribution” and “retaliation.”

“He is not going to stop with a case that gets thrown out, because the U.S. attorney didn’t work the grand jury correctly, and he’s not going to stop because this case will ultimately fail on its own lack of merit as well,” McCabe claimed.

“This, this campaign against those people he thinks are enemies will continue until he is gone,” he continued. “Jim Comey knows that as well as anyone, and the rest of us are going to just have to sit here and endure it.”

Comey maintained his innocence in a Tuesday video responding to the indictment, insisting he was “not afraid.”

“Well, they’re back. This time about a picture of seashells on a North Carolina Beach a year ago, and this won’t be the end of it,” he said. “But nothing has changed with me. I’m still innocent, I’m still not afraid, and I still believe in the independent federal judiciary. So let’s go.”

data.title: House Republican on Comey indictment 'There's something called dangerous speak'
data.description:
Rep. Dan Meuser (R-Pa.) on Tuesday criticized former FBI Director James Comey after he was indicted by a grand jury in a new case brought by the Department of Justice, the second time DOJ has sought to prosecute him.

The new lawsuit concerns a social media post Comey made last year that involved seashells in the sand that were arranged to form the numbers 86 and 47.

“Hey, look, you know, when the former FBI director writes in the sand, 86-47, which means, kill the 47th president, that’s concerning. So, see what the intent is? You know something, Blake? There’s something called dangerous speak. Dangerous speak means it’s worse than hate speak,” Meuser told NewsNation’s Blake Burman on “The Hill.”

“It means it invokes in others the desire to carry out violent acts. And what James Comey put out, yeah, that could certainly be interpreted to mean to carry out violent acts,” he added.

Comey last May posted a photo of seashells on a beach set up to display the numbers 86-47, which President Trump, the 47th president, said at the time was a call for his killing.

“He knew exactly what that meant. A child knows what that meant. If you’re the FBI director and you don’t know what that meant, that meant assassination. And it says it loud and clear,” Trump told Fox News last year.

Comey is facing two charges for allegedly threatening the president via the seashells post, per the indictment.

“The defendant, James Brien Comey Jr., did knowingly and willfully make a threat to take the life of, and to inflict bodily harm upon, the President of the United States,” the indictment reads.

data.title: Live updates: Hegseth to rail against 'open-ended' wars in House hearing
data.description:
The House Armed Services Committee has its chance to question Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Joint Chiefs of Staff Chair Gen. Dan Caine on Wednesday morning amid the Iran war as it hits 60 days with no imminent conclusion. It’s their first of two days of hearings.

According to a copy of his opening remarks, Hegseth with rail against “open-ended” wars pursued by past administrations, as the U.S. war in Iran approached the 60-day mark and Trump signaling a continued standoff in the Strait of Hormuz.

“For a generation, the United States was largely distracted by open-ended wars, regime change, and nation building,” Hegseth’s remarks say. “These costly diversions lacked clearly defined goals and were not tied to vital and core American interests. President Trump is changing that.”

Hegseth is likely to face questions from both sides over Trump’s plans to bring an end to the Iran war, and what exactly the U.S. hopes to achieve when it’s over.

The Supreme Court will hand down opinions before holding its final oral arguments of the term, including weighing the Trump administration’s efforts to curtail temporary legal protections for hundreds of thousands of noncitizens.

On Capitol Hill, Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) is coming under pressure from Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) and others to schedule a vote immediately on a bill to authorize $400 billion for the White House ballroom after Saturday’s shooting at the White House Correspondents’ dinner. But not everyone is on board, as affordability — and midterm election — concerns swirl.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will hold what is likely his final news conference at 2:30 p.m., following the Fed interest rate decision. His expected successor, Kevin Warsh, faces a confirmation vote in the Senate Banking Committee earlier in the day.

Trump on Wednesday will greet the Artemis II astronauts at the White House.

Follow live below.

data.title: Raskin: Comey charges 'surreal and absurd'
data.description:
Rep. Jamie Raskin (D-Md.) on Tuesday called the new indictment charges against former FBI Director James Comey “surreal and absurd.”

Comey’s second indictment is connected to a social media photo he posted last May of several seashells on a beach in North Carolina arranged to form “86-47.” The Department of Justice (DOJ), referring to the post, announced the same day that Comey faces two counts of making threats to harm President Trump.

“I mean, the charges here are just surreal and absurd,” Raskin said on MS NOW’s “All In with Chris Hayes.” “But of course, from the perspective of the person who’s obviously calling the shots and proudly calling the shots under the unitary executive theory of the Justice Department in America, you know, it’s not important whether or not the grand jury either, you know, indicts or doesn’t indict or whether the court throws it out or doesn’t throw it out or the person is convicted or not.”

Raskin questioned why these charges were being issued now, calling it “odd for somebody to threaten the life of the president and for nobody to notice for half a year, or something like that.”

“Look Chris, I think every reasonable person left in America understands that this is not an act of law enforcement and justice,” Raskin said. “This is an act of political vendetta, and the Department of Justice has been turned into an instrument of political revenge against President Trump’s perceived foes.”

The Maryland Democrat later added that the new charges will “occupy” Comey’s time and financial resources, and that “occupying other people’s time and space, that’s all [Trump] cares about.”

“It’s not like he’s got some program for America he’s working on, like he’s focused in the same way on trying to get health care to everybody or lower the price of groceries, or lower the price of gasoline, or end the war that he just unleashed on the world in Iran,” Raskin added. “He’s focused on Mr. Comey and what happened at the beach nine months ago.”

Last May, Trump accused Comey of knowing “exactly” what the post allegedly meant, that it was a call for his assassination.

The DOJ on Tuesday interpreted the seashell arrangement as something that “a reasonable recipient who is familiar with the circumstances would interpret as a serious expression of an intent to do harm to the President of the United States.”

The phrase “86” is commonly used as slang for removing or throwing out something or someone. Comey later clarified that he assumed the shells were a “political message,” but he said he didn’t realize the numbers could be associated with violence. He added that he opposes all forms of violence and deleted the post.

Comey denied any wrongdoing and declared his innocence in a short video clip posted to Substack on Tuesday.

The indictment was announced two days after a shooting erupted at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner, which Trump attended for the first time between his two presidencies. A 31-year-old California man, Cole Allen, was charged in connection with an attempt to assassinate the president.
